title: Option trading with AI
author: Cool_Ease_8553
selftext:
Looking for recommendations on platforms, GitHub repos, APIs, or educational resources. Also curious how you’re doing backtesting for options strategies since it seems way more complex than equities (historical chains, IV, spreads, execution assumptions, etc.).

Would love to hear any ideas even if they sound crazy 😂

comments.author: BuildwithPublic
comments.body:
Options backtesting is genuinely harder than most people admit - historical IV surfaces, realistic spread assumptions, and execution slippage all compound the complexity. Anyone claiming their backtest is 1:1 with live performance is probably overfitting.

What's actually useful:

For historical chains - CBOE DataShop and Nasdaq Data Link have the cleanest data but it's not cheap. OptionsDX is a more affordable starting point for retail.

For frameworks -vectorbt is solid for Python-based options backtesting, more flexible than Backtrader for multi-leg strategies.

For live execution once you've validated a strategy - r/PublicApp 's API is worth looking at. Free key, one click, stocks/options/crypto/bonds all supported. We also have an MCP server so you can connect Claude directly to your live account for strategy iteration and execution. Happy to help if you go that route — just DM me

comments.author: NoBlood8896
comments.body:
For options, I would be careful using AI anywhere near execution at first.



The useful lane is more like: summarize the chain, compare IV/rank/event context, sanity-check the spread assumptions, then force you to write the thesis and invalidation before entry.



Backtesting gets ugly fast because fills, IV surface changes, assignment risk, and spread liquidity matter. So I’d start with decision support + post-trade review before trying to let an LLM “trade.”



I’m building ATLAS in that direction: AI around context/review/risk, not a magic entry button.



Free launch here if useful: [https://theatlasterminal.com/free-launch](https://theatlasterminal.com/free-launch)

replies.author: Cool_Ease_8553
replies.body: Interesting… Will definitely take a look 👍🏼

comments.author: fibspeak
comments.body:
honestly id recommend not. 

long options is the hardest game in the market. its not easily solved with software. 

short options is the most risky game in the market. it works well most of the time and wipes you out in one. 

  
neither of these are game to play without a lot of practice.
